nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing rocky and gravelly rivers and streams in hill and foothills zones; also found in slower tributaries and floodplain channels during monsoon season. Prefers areas with abundant cover — submerged roots, leaf litter, and boulders.

decorPreferences: smooth rounded river pebbles and gravel substrate
driftwood caves and tangles for shelter
robust rooted plants (Java fern, Anubias, Microsorum) anchored to hardscape
dim areas or overhangs to replicate shaded river margins

diet.notes: The Bengal Loach is a strongly snail-eating, insect-leaning omnivore and an effective natural controller of pest snails such as ramshorns and bladder snails. The core of its diet in the aquarium should be high-quality sinking pellets or wafers, supplemented with frozen foods such as bloodworms, tubifex, daphnia, and brine shrimp. Blanched vegetables — zucchini, cucumber, or spinach — are generally accepted, and algae wafers help round out plant-matter intake. Live and frozen snails of all sizes are relished. Because the species is nocturnal, feeding after lights-out best matches its natural foraging schedule.

goodWith: other Botiidae loaches (kept in separate species groups)
avoidWith: dwarf shrimp (neocaridina, caridina) — will be eaten
very small fish under 3 cm (fry risk)
snails intended to be kept (consumed readily)
small peaceful bottom-dwellers that compete for hiding spots

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase photo record for Botia dario. Check FishBase species page for current image availability and license details.

summary: The Bengal Loach (Botia dario) is a boldly striped, social loach from northeastern India and Bangladesh that reaches 12–15 cm. It needs to be kept in groups of at least five, is a highly effective snail eater, and is an active, personable addition to mid-to-large community tanks with good water flow.
careNotes:
The Bengal Loach is native to the Brahmaputra and Ganges river drainages of northeastern India (West Bengal, Assam) and Bangladesh, where it inhabits fast-flowing, oxygen-rich rivers and hill streams. The species belongs to the family Botiidae, which was long treated as a subfamily of the broader loach family Cobitidae but is now widely recognised as a distinct family in its own right. It is closely related to and frequently confused with the Zebra Loach (Botia striata) and the Comb-tail Loach (Botia histrionica); Botia dario can be distinguished by its more numerous and irregular body stripes, which extend well onto the fins.

This is a species that should always be kept in a group of at least five. Solitary or pair-kept specimens tend to become stressed, stay hidden, and often show increased susceptibility to disease. Larger groups of six to ten or more bring out richer social behaviour — play-chasing, mock sparring, and synchronised foraging. Some intragroup squabbling is normal and generally harmless provided the tank is large enough (a 120 cm footprint is the recommended minimum) and plenty of caves, driftwood, and other hiding places are available. Toward robust mid-water tankmates, Bengal Loaches tend to be peaceful.

Good oxygenation and moderate water movement are important; an additional powerhead or spray bar is often recommended. The temperature should be kept between 22 and 27 °C, as values below 20 °C or above 28 °C are considered stressful. Soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral water — pH 6.5–7.5, gH 5–15 — suits the species well. Like most loaches, Bengal Loaches are sensitive to deteriorating water quality, so regular water changes of 25–30% per week are advised.

Botia dario has a sub-orbital spine (a sharpened extension of the cheekbone) that can erect when the fish is stressed. Fine-mesh nets should be used to avoid entanglement, and handling should be kept to a minimum. Clicking sounds produced by the loach’s pharyngeal teeth (the tooth-like structures in its throat) are normal social communication and generally indicate a settled, comfortable group.

For pest snail control, Bengal Loaches tend to be highly effective, systematically working through ramshorn, bladder, and pond snail populations. They should not be kept with any snails intended as display animals.
breedingNotes: Breeding Botia dario in captivity is rarely achieved and has not been reliably documented under standard aquarium conditions. In the wild, spawning is believed to be triggered by monsoon flooding — a dramatic drop in water temperature, increased flow, and a reduction in hardness associated with seasonal rainfall. Commercial fish farms in India and Bangladesh produce the species for the trade using hormone-induced spawning to mimic these monsoon triggers, but this approach is not accessible to most hobbyists. Attempting to simulate monsoon conditions through a series of cool water changes, elevated flow, and reduced hardness may encourage spawning behaviour in a well-conditioned group, but verified fry production by home aquarists is extremely rare.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Botia dario
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (India/Bangladesh, Brahmaputra/Ganges drainages), adult size (to 15 cm), temperature (22–26°C), pH (6.5–7.5), hardness, substrate preferences, social requirements (groups of 5+), diet, and taxonomy. Botiidae family placement and distinction from B. striata and B. histrionica confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Botia dario
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (reported to 15 cm TL), native range (Ganges and Brahmaputra drainages, NE India and Bangladesh), taxonomy (Botiidae, Cypriniformes), temperature range, and diet (invertebrates, detritus, plant matter). Confirmed classification within Botiidae.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Loach Care Guide
additional.notes: General loach husbandry guidance used for shoaling requirements, snail-eating behaviour, tank setup (caves, driftwood), and feeding recommendations (sinking foods, snails as supplement). Confirmed group-size minimum of 5+ and semi-aggressive temperament within species.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Botia dario profile
additional.notes: Species profile used for confirmation of common name (Bengal Loach / Queen Loach), natural habitat description (fast-flowing rocky rivers, NE India), social behaviour notes, and snail predation. Confirmed intermediate care level and minimum tank size recommendations.

additional.name: Loaches Online — Botia dario species profile
additional.notes: Specialist loach resource. Used for sub-orbital spine care note, clicking behaviour as social communication, confusion with B. striata and B. histrionica, intragroup social dynamics, and monsoon-breeding hypothesis. Confirmed breeding as extremely rare in captivity without hormones.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable home-aquarium breeding reported. Commercial production uses hormone injection to mimic monsoon spawning triggers. Attempting monsoon simulation (cool water change, increased flow, reduced hardness) may induce spawning behaviour but verified fry production by hobbyists is extremely rare.
